title: I Quit Chrome for an AI Browser. It Actually Worked.
transcript: I've been using Google Chrome for 14 years. Every browser I tried to switch to, Brave, Arc, Firefox, even Safari, I came back to Chrome within a week. This [music] week I tried something different, an AI native browser built by Norton. And for the first time, I didn't switch back. This is what changed. Here's what my work day actually looks like. Right now I have 37 tabs open. Documentation in three of them, sponsored email drafts in two, >> [music] >> five tabs of research I started for a video and forgot about. ChatGPT in one, Claude in another, Gemini in a third because I'm comparing them, and the browser [music] is just a passive container for all of this. It doesn't know what I'm doing. It doesn't help. Most browsers were built for 2010. We're not browsing [music] the web anymore, we're working in it. There's a difference. And after a week with Norton Neo, I think it's the only browser that actually understands that. Norton Neo is an AI native browser. The refresh release just dropped and it's loaded. It's got built-in VPN, anti-fingerprinting protection, improved ad [music] blocking, phishing alerts on emails, vertical tabs, split view, custom magic feeds, a few different magic box modes for how the AI surfaces context, and mobile apps. We'll get into each of these. The thing that makes it different from Chrome with a ChatGPT extension is the AI lives inside the browser itself with full context of what you're looking at across every tab. It's also built by Norton. Yes, that Norton, the antivirus one, which matters more than I expected. Privacy and tracker blocking aren't an afterthought here. They're the foundation. More on that in a minute. So, let's get to the first use case. Yesterday I was researching whether agentic SEO actually works on dead websites. Five articles open. I asked the AI sidebar, "Summarize the key claims across all five tabs. Mark what you're sourced and what you're speculation." It read all five tabs, pulled the cited claims into one column, and unsourced ones into another. Saved me maybe 40 minutes of skimming. The thing I want to point out, see how each claim links back to which tab it came from? I can verify before I quote it. That's the difference between AI that helps you ship and AI that helps you embarrass yourself. All right, use case two, tab management. Three pieces. Vertical tabs alone is a productivity multiplier. I can actually read the tab titles instead of guessing from a 16 pixel favicon. Split view. I can run the AI sidebar reading documentation on the left while I write code in a tab on the right. No alt tab, no second monitor. And AI tab groups. When I'm in my video script group, the AI only pulls contacts from those four tabs. When I switch to sponsor emails, it forgets the video research and only sees the sponsor pipeline. That's not a Chrome feature. That's a productivity multiplier. Use case three is where Norton's heritage actually shows up. The refresh release ships with four privacy features that Chrome users have to bolt on with extensions. And even then, they break things. One, VPN comes built-in. And here's the unlock. If you set Neo as your default browser, your free VPN allotment doubles. Free. No second subscription. The privacy company knows what they're doing. Two is anti-fingerprinting. Most trackers don't use cookies anymore. They fingerprint your browser. Screen size, fonts, plugins, all combine into a unique ID. Neo masks this by default. I checked on coveryourtracks.eff.org, and Neo password Chrome failed. Three, improved ad blocking and tracker blocking. I ran the same dev tools test on five sites in Chrome and Neo. Chrome loaded 187 trackers across those sites. Neo blocked all but 12, the functional ones I actually need. Faster page loads, no third-party fingerprinting, no consent banners hijacking my screen. Four, fishing alerts for emails. When I open a Gmail tab and there's a suspicious link, Neo flags it before I click, not after. This is Norton applying 30 years of fishing detection to the browser layer. As a creator who gets 100 sponsor emails a week, half of them with sketchy attachments, this is the feature I didn't know I needed. Norton has been doing tracker analysis for 30 years. They're not learning this from scratch. You don't have to install three privacy extensions and pray they don't break the site. The AI sidebar in Neo has different magic modes. Research mode for cross-tab synthesis, summary mode for page summarization with citations, query mode for open-ended chat. Each mode is tuned differently. Most browsers ship you one AI assistant. Neo lets you pick the right mode for the work you're doing right now. Custom magic feed. This is a personalized stream of content the AI curates based on what you've been working on. It's not a generic newsfeed. It's context-aware. If I've been researching AI agents all week, my feed surfaces the latest papers, blog posts, and tools in that space. It's like an RSS reader that actually pays attention. Page summarization with citation accuracy. When I asked Neo a question I didn't have the data for, it told me it didn't hallucinate. That's [music] the difference between a browser AI built on top of a search engine versus one that knows the limits of its sources. Okay, let me be honest about where I think it falls short. One, it's still new. Some integrations I use daily, Notion, Linear, GitHub Codespaces, those work fine, but I had to manually log them in on day one. No Chrome profile import yet. And [music] the second thing is that the AI is excellent in synthesis and search, but it's It's a coding agent. Don't expect it to replace cursor or your IDE assistant. That's not what it's built for and that's fine. Three is the keyboard shortcuts are mostly Chrome compatible, but a couple were different. Took me half a day to retrain my muscle memory. But look, none of these are deal breakers. They're just real. And the reason I haven't switched back to Chrome is because I keep finding things the AI does that I didn't think I needed. Yesterday I was writing this script and it pulled in citations from a tab I'd had open for 3 days. I had completely forgotten that tab existed. The browser remembered it for me. And that's the shift. A browser used to be infrastructure, Norton Neo is closer to a coworker. And the part that surprised me is that Norton of all companies is the one that built it, not Google, not OpenAI, not a startup nobody's heard of, the privacy company. And that actually tracks if you think about it. They're the ones with the least incentive to sell your data to anyone. Also worth knowing, Neo has mobile apps now, iOS and Android. So the workflow follows me. Same tab groups, same magic feeds, same VPN. I've been using it on my phone for 3 days and the handoff between desktop and mobile actually works. If you want to try it, link is in the description, neobrowser.ai. Free download takes about 30 seconds. Works [music] on Mac, Windows, Linux, plus iOS and Android. Set Neo as your default browser and your free VPN allotment doubles. >> [music] >> Give it a week the way I did and notice what you start doing differently. If you enjoyed this video, please hit the like button and subscribe for more AI tool tests. Try out Neo and let me know your experience in the comment section. I will be reading them. For now, I've got to go ungoogle my life, so thanks for watching.
